strings.tag: Hi [name], this is a test string from room003.
It looks like you've got 7 pieces of evidence. How can I help you?
Click and put any piece of evidence to the correct processing station for analysis. Or click and then put the evidence to your P.D.A and it will tell you what to do with it.
Are you sure? You need to check if there is still evidence that needs to be analyzed.
Yes, I agree. The next step will be to collect a hair sample, a DNA sample and a bitemark impression from each of the three suspects.
How do I process evidence?
I am ready to process evidence.
I think I am done here.
I'll continue my analysis.
I'll select my next location.
Back already? How can I help you?
I am ready to process more evidence.
The Fingerprint Station has all the powders and chemicals you need to process any item for fingerprints. You can also use the computer to search for prints.
There is a better station to use to analyze that evidence. Try using it somewhere else.
You've analyzed this evidence. The fingerprints on the spoon matched to a dog breeder named Wesley Murphy.
We'll use the "Superglue Fuming Method" to check for fingerprints. It can detect even the faintest prints. First, set up the experiment. It will only take 10 minutes or so to develop the prints.

Fumes from the evaporating superglue react with amino acids and oils left behind on everything we touch. The reaction produces a white, sticky material, and makes the fingerprint easy to see. I'll scan this print into the computer for you to analyze.
What did you learn from the fingerprint comparison?
Mr. Murphy left his fingerprints at the crime scene, but we don't know yet if he murdered Marco Williams. Try again.
Mr. Murphy left his fingerprints at the crime scene, but we don't know if he took Apollo away. Try again.
How did Wesley Murphy's fingerprints get into the IAFIS Fingerprint Database?
That's not what I read from the IAFIS database. Try again.
Nope. We don't have every American's fingerprints in the database. Try again.
What technique did you use to develop the fingerprints on the spoon?
No, that's not the case this time. Try again.
According to the dog show records, Wesley Murphy has a dog competing in the show against Apollo. Make sure you talk with him.
Someone named Wesley Murphy murdered Marco Williams.
Someone named Wesley Murhpy kidnapped Apollo.
Someone named Wesley Murphy left his fingerprints on a spoon at the crime scene.
Wesley Murphy was arrested for drunk driving.
Wesley Murphy was fingerprinted for a background search.
Every American's fingerprint is in the IAFIS databse.
I brushed the spoon with fingerprint powder.
I used the Superglue Fuming method.
The fingerprints were naturally visible on the surface of the spoon.
I'll talk to him later.
The Forensic Toxicology Station detects the presence of drugs and other chemicals in any substance.
There is a better station to use to analyze that evidence. Try using it somewhere else.
You've analyzed this evidence.

This dog food smells like bitter almonds, and that suggests CYANIDE (SIGH-uh-nide). Cyanide is a deadly poison for humans and animals. Use FORENSIC TOXICOLOGY to detect cyanide in this dog food. The procedure is complicated, so I'll walk you through it.
Assuming there is cyanide in the dog food, can you predict the color change in each of the wells?
No, only the wells that contain cyanide will react with the reagent and turn blue. Try again.
Only the wells that contain cyanide will turn blue. The negative control contains just water, not cyanide. Try again.
What does the positive control tell you?
No. If that were the case, all the fluids would be blue. Try again.
Remember, we added cyanide as the positive control. It's the cyanide that turns the reagent blue. Try again.
Correct. Now, why didn't the "extraction buffer" liquid change colors?
Remember -- no blue means no cyanide. Try again.
No, you added reagent to all the wells on the testing tray. Try again.
You are right. Final question -- what does this experiment tell you about Apollo's dog food?
Are you sure? If the liquid turned blue, that means there's cyanide present. Try again.
Arsenic? Where did that come from? This is a test for cyanide! Try again.
Well done. I wonder who would want to poison Apollo -- and if Marco Williams knew about it!
The sample and positive control wells will turn blue.
The extraction buffer and negative controls wells will turn blue.
All the wells will turn blue.
The positive control proves this test makes all chemicals turn blue.
The positive control proves this test detects cyanide.
The positive control proves the color blue means no cyanide is present.
Because the extraction buffer contains cyanide.
Because the extraction buffer doesn't contain cyanide.
Because the reagent wasn't added to that well.
The dog food contained cyanide.
The dog food did not contain cyanide.
The dog food contained arsenic.
I'll keep investigating.
You've analyzed this evidence.

So, Doctor Robbins thinks our victim may have been injected with a chemical substance. Maybe it's some type of drug. I'll go ahead to run an Enzyme-Linked ImmunoSorbent Assay (ELISA) as a screening test. You can help me interpret the results.
Here's what the results look like. Let's see how much you remember from Rookie Training. What is a "positive control?"
Try again. The purpose of the positive control is to make sure the test can detect the drug when present.
There's no charge on any of these samples. A positive control is used to make sure the test can detect each drug. Try again.
Good. Now, why is each test duplicated?
Actually, this makes the experiment cost twice as much. But, we do it for a very important reason. Know why?
No -- every blood sample on here came from Marco Williams. Try again.
Correct. Last question -- By looking at the result, what drug is present in Marco Williams' blood?
No, if there were amphetamines in his blood, the blood sample would look like the positive control. Try again.
Try again. You're looking for the test where the blood sample is identical to the positive control.

The screening test detected some kind of barbiturates, so we'll use Gas Chromatography-Mass Spectrometry (GC/MS) to confirm exactly what chemical is present. The machine is already set up to test the victim's blood.
According to the mass spectrum generated from the GC/MS, we found a match in our database -- Sodium thiopental.

A mass spectrum is a graph displaying the different properties of a specific chemical. Every chemical has its own unique mass spectrum. That's why we can confirm that sodium thiopental is in the victim's blood sample.
Sodium thiopental -- also known as "truth serum." This is a type of BARBITURATE that's used to induce comas and for lethal injections.
Enough to knock Mr. Williams unconscious, but not enough to kill him outright. I wonder how this is related to the blow that killed him?
1. The chemicals in the sample separate from each other based on the difference in their properties.
2. In the ion source, the chemical gets a positive (+) charge and breaks into fragments.
3. The analyzer sorts the fragments of each chemical by similar properties.
4. The detector adds up the chemical fragments of each type.
5. Sending these data to the computer.
Generating a graph of these data (called mass spectrum)...
Matching mass spectrum in the database...
A sample that has a positive charge.
A sample that doesn't contain any drugs.
A sample that purposely contains a certain drug.
To save on money.
Each test sample comes from a different victim.
To help ensure no mistakes are made.
Amphetamines.
Barbiturates (bar-BICH-ur-its).
There were no drugs in his blood stream.
What is a mass spectrum?
What is sodium thiopental?
How much sodium thiopental was in his body?
I'll continue my investigation.
The Comparison Microscope is normally used to compare two objects. It can also be used to look at a single object in detail.
There is a better station to analyze that evidence. Try analyzing it somewhere else.
You can compare the MORPHOLOGY (the structure and form) of this hair with the unknown human hair. If they are close enough, we'll use mtDNA testing to confirm the match.
You've analyzed this evidence.
You've analyzed this evidence.

I've prepared the hair for microscopic examination. Our first step will be to identify this as human hair, dog hair, or something else entirely. Before you do that, you'll need to know something about the hair structure.
Based on what you saw under the microscope, what would you conclude?
Are you sure? The absence of a medulla is common in human hairs, but not animals. Try again.
The hair has imbricate scale pattern and no medulla. It's telling you something. Try again.
Exactly. If you collect hair from the suspects, we can try to identify whose hair it is. Let's examine the hair again and see if we can test it for DNA.
No follicular tag means no nuclear DNA, but 93% of hairs contain mitochondrial DNA. We can test for that.
Nuclear DNA is carried inside the nucleus of the cell. There are only two copies per cell, and no two individuals (except identical twins) have the same nuclear DNA.
Mitochondria are small ORGANELLES that power the cell. Each cell can contain thousands of mitochondria, so there's thousands of copies of mitochondrial DNA per cell.
To compare mitochondrial DNA, or mtDNA, we have to sequence it. And since that's expensive and time consuming, we can't compare the mtDNA of every suspect.
Collect hair samples from the suspects and compare them to the hair found at the crime scene. If we find a match, then we'll confirm it with a mtDNA comparison.
Wait, let's make sure we are on the same page first. What did you learn from this unidentified hair?
Apollo? The missing dog? I don't think so. Try again.
Wrong species. The scale pattern is dog-like, but the medulla is not. Try again.
How do you know it's human? What characteristics did you look at?
No -- many people and many dogs can have hair of this length and color. Try something more scientific.
That's not what I recall. We looked at the follicle to see if there was nuclear DNA, that's all. To determine the species, we looked at two different structures.
Correct. Final question -- what should you do next?
Are you sure? Remember, there's no follicle attached, so there's no nuclear DNA to analyze. Try something else.
Close, but no. Testing the mtDNA is too expensive to do for every suspect, so we'll use a preliminary test first to narrow the list.
Correct. Remember to collect samples from the suspects who have similar hair, and bring them back here to compare.
The hair found at the crime scene is an animal hair.
The hair found at the crime scene is a human hair.
I can't tell.
Sure.
What's nuclear DNA?
What's mitochondrial DNA?
How do I compare mitochondrial DNA?
When will we compare mtDNA sequences?
Okay. I'm ready to continue my investigation.
The hair is from Apollo.
The hair is from an unknown dog.
The hair is from an unknown human.
Its length and color.
Its scale pattern and medulla.
Its cortex and follicle.
I should analyze every suspect's nuclear DNA.
I should analyze every suspect's mitochondrial DNA.
I should find the suspect that has a similar hair sample.
I'll add collecting hair samples to my to-do list.
I agree, Ms. Ranger isn't our unknown hair donor. She's not even a natural blond -- you can see her original hair color near the root. Keep looking for more hair samples.
I think we have a match! Sariel Knox's hair is the same texture and color as the unknown sample. So, what should you do next?
Are you sure? Remember, there is no follicular tag attached to the hair found at the crime scene, and no tag means no nuclear DNA. Try again.
Correct. You'll have to run a confirmatory test comparing the mtDNA from the crime scene hair to Dr. Knox to make a definite identification.
I have to run a mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA) comparison to confirm it.
I have to run a nuclear DNA comparison to confirm it.
I'll do that when I'm finished here.
The Light Table is very useful for analyzing and comparing pieces of evidence in detail.
There is a better station to analyze that evidence. Try analyzing it somewhere else.
You've analyzed this evidence.

First, we'll determine if this bitemark was made by a human or a dog -- both are likely suspects in this case. If it was created by a human, we can then compare it to bite impressions from your suspects.
To measure the bitemark, we use the ABFO No. 2 scale, a tool designed by the American Board of Forensic Odontology.

So, a human bit Marco Williams before he died? That's an interesting bit of evidence. Start collecting bite impressions from the people involved in the case. Hopefully, we can find a match -- or at least eliminate people as suspects.
Roll over the words to compare human and canine teeth.
You've analyzed this evidence.

So you think there are some indented markings left on this note pad? Let's check it out. Luckily, I have just the tool to reveal them -- an Electrostatic Detection Apparatus! We call it the ESDA for short.

Our trusty ESDA machine is commonly used to reveal indented impressions on paper which may otherwise go unnoticed. I'll explain how it works when we are doing the analysis. Sometimes we can read three, four or even more layers below the original writing.
Which is the newest impression on this paper?
No, I can read quite a few of the words here. Look harder and try again.
No -- newer impressions are usually deeper and collect more of the powder, so they're usually the darkest. Try again.
The paper is illegible.
The meeting note is the newer impression.
The to-do list is the newer impression.
Place the object in the fuming chamber.
Add Superglue to the warming tray.
Place the warming tray on the hot plate inside the fuming chamber.
Close the door and turn on the machine.
The bottom of the machine serves as the hot plate and it heats up to the appropriate temperature.

Fumes from the evaporating superglue react with amino acids and oils left behind on everything we touch. The reaction produces a white, sticky material, and makes the fingerprint easy to see. I'll scan this print into the computer for you to analyze.

To find out if the dog food contains cyanide, we need to EXTRACT (remove) it from the dog food. The test tube contains EXTRACTION BUFFER -- a chemical that dissolves any cyanide in the dog food. Now, put some dog food in the test tube there.
Then use the pipette to transfer the dissolved dog food from the test tube to the SAMPLE well on our testing tray.

To make sure the extraction buffer isn't the chemical that is reacting, we'll test some of it that wasn't exposed to the dog food. Here is a pipette with a new tip to avoid contamination. Use it to transfer extraction buffer from the other test tube to the EXTRACTION BUFFER well.

In addition, scientists use POSITIVE CONTROLS (samples that purposely contain a certain drug) and NEGATIVE CONTROLS (samples that purposely do NOT have the drug) to ensure the test is working correctly.
Cyanide is used as a positive control in our test. Use the pipette with a new tip to add the cyanide solution to the well marked POSITIVE CONTROL.
We'll use pure water for the negative control. Use the pipette with a new tip to transfer some water to the well labeled NEGATIVE CONTROL.

Next we'll add a reagent (ree-A-gent), a chemical that detects other chemicals by reacting with them. This reaction produces a color. In this case, we'll use a reagent that reacts with cyanide to produce a blue color.
Correct. Now, use the pipette with a new tip to add reagent to EACH of the wells and we'll see the results.
SAMPLE
EXTRACTION BUFFER
POSITIVE CONTROL
NEGATIVE CONTROL
Using the scale in the photograph, what is the approximate width of the bitemark?
Look at the scale beneath the bitemark. Use it to approximate the width and be sure to use the correct unit!
Using the same scale, what is the approximate height of the bitemark?
Try again. The scale in this photograph is in centimeters.
What type of bitemark is this -- human or canine?
This bitemark is more circular, and the canine teeth impressions don't stand out much. Try again.
Human Teeth
Canine Teeth
Incisors
Used for cutting off bite-sized portions. Humans have 8. Dogs have 12.
Canine
Used for holding prey, slashing, and tearing. Humans have 4. Dogs have 4.
Bicuspids

Used for holding food and breaking it into small pieces.
Humans have 8.
Dogs have 16.
Molars
Used to grind food into small pieces. Humans have 12. Dogs have 10.
Compare Bitemarks
Anterior Teeth

The anterior teeth consist of incisors and canine.
These front-most teeth usually produce a bitemark.
Human Bitemark
Canine Bitemark
6 Anterior Teeth that usually appear in a bitemark
8 Anterior Teeth that usually appear in a bitemark
Small Canine Teeth
Large, prominent Canines
Bitemark is roughly circular
Bitemark is oval shaped
1 centimeter
1 inch
4 centimeters
4 inches
2 centimeters
2 inches
3.5 centimeters
3.5 inches

Bitemarks aren't as unique as fingerprints or DNA -- people can have very similar teeth. However, bitemarks can be used to rule out suspects. We'll use this impression to create a 3D cast of the suspect's teeth. We'll then use a TRANSPARENT OVERLAY METHOD to compare it to the victim's bitemark photograph.
You've analyzed this evidence.
So, who or what caused the bitemark on Marco Williams' arm?
No, the first thing we did was determine if the bite was caused by a human or a dog -- and it was definitely a human bite. Try again.
Not quite. Remember, bitemarks are usually used to rule out suspects. On its own, a bitemark is usually not enough to convict someone.
I agree, Dr. Knox probably bit the victim. How can we confirm it?
No, I don't think that will help us.
Her dental records won't help us in this situation.
Correct. The DNA will confirm whether she was the biter.
Sariel Knox probably bit him.
Sariel Knox definitely bit him.
Apollo bit him.
We can have her bite someone else and compare the result.
We can match the DNA from the bitemark saliva with her DNA.
We can look at her dental records.
I'll go analyze that after I am done here.
You have some experience casting bite impressions. I can handle this one, if you want.
We need casts from both bottom and top teeth. Let's begin with the top one. First, mix the dental stone with the water in the bowl.
Now, put the impression on the vibrator and turn it on.
Carefully pour the dental stone mix into the impression. The vibrator can help remove bubbles when you pour the mix into the impression.
We need to let it set before we can remove the cast. So, let's repeat the steps to finish the bottom teeth.
Mix the dental stone with the water in the bowl.
Now, put the impression on the vibrator and turn it on.
Carefully pour the dental stone mix into the impression.
Good job! Now, we need to wait until the dental stone hardens.
The casts are hardened and I've made them ready for you. Now, you're going to create two transparent overlays from these casts to compare them to the victim's bitemark photograph.
I've placed a transparent sheet on top of this dental cast. Click each of the six anterior teeth to trace its outline onto the sheet.
Now, label the actual left and right side of the biter's impression on the sheet. Remember, the biter's impression is the mirror image of both the cast and the outline.
Well done. Now repeat the procedure with the cast of the suspect's bottom teeth.
This is not correct. When looking at the outline, the right side of the biter's impression is on your left, and the left side is on your right. Try again.
Now, label the actual left and right side of the biter's impression on the sheet. Again, the biter's impression is the mirror image of both the cast and the outline.
Well done. Click both tracings to switch the left and right sides and determine if there is a match or not.
I don't think so -- there are substantial differences between the tracings and the bitemark. Look closely at the details and try again.
Are you sure? They look pretty similar. Look closer and try again.
It looks like Deborah Ranger's teeth didn't create this bitemark.
It looks like Wesley Murphy's teeth didn't create this bitemark.
Do it myself
Get Greg to do it
Murphy's bite impression
Ranger's bite impression
Knox's bite impression

Print Added

This new print source has been added to the computer for comparison with the prints found during the investigation.
Drag a fingerprint to a "Viewing area" to begin.
Identify as many ridge characteristics in the fingerprint as possible.
Search the database.
Select a second fingerprint for comparison.

Class Characteristics

Is this fingerprint a loop, whorl, or arch?
Scanned fingerprints:
Possible matches:
Loop
Whorl
Arch

Class Characteristics of Fingerprints

Class Characteristics of fingerprints are shared by an entire group or type of fingerprints. For example, there are three main classes of fingerprints:

LOOP
The ridges enter and exit the same side of the finger

WHORL
The ridges form a circular or spiral pattern

ARCH
The ridges run from one side of the finger to the other
Individual Characteristics of Fingerprints
Individual Characteristics are unique to specific, individual fingerprints. These are also called ridge characteristics or minutia.
Common Ridge Characteristics
BIFURCATION
ISLAND (SHORT RIDGE)
HOOK (SPUR)
LAKE (ENCLOSURE)
RIDGE ENDING
Bifurcation
Island
Hook
Lake
Ridge Ending
IAFIS

The Integrated Automated Fingerprint Identification System (IAFIS) is the FBI's national fingerprint database. It contains over 470,000,000 fingerprints. On average, it takes about two hours to search the entire database.
Match Detected!

Name: Wesley Murphy
Occupation: Dog Breeder
Address: 4675 48th Street, Apt 404, Houston, TX

Fingerprint Source:
Arrest for DUI Oct. 6, 2000
MATCH
NOT A MATCH
HELP
SEARCH
SUBMIT
BEGIN
DONE
START OVER
GET HELP
Tell me more
Remove the top sheet of the note pad and place it on the ESDA.
Cover the paper with a thin clear plastic film to protect the evidence.
Turn on the vacuum.
Use the wand to apply a positive charge to the paper.
Sprinkle the paper with the negatively charged black powder from the bottle.
Let's see what's going on. First, you covered the evidence with a thin piece of plastic. The vacuum from the ESDA pulled the plastic into the indentations on the paper.
When the wand is waved over the paper, it carries a strong electric field. This gives the paper a positive static charge, which accumulates in the indentations in the paper.
The toner particles have a negative charge. When they're applied to the paper, they are attracted to the positive charge in the indentations and stick there.
The final result looks something like this. The darker text comes from deeper, more recent impression. The lighter text is from shallower impressions -- these are probably older.
The DNA Station processes evidence that contains DNA, such as hair, blood, saliva, sweat. Using the computer, you can search DNA samples in CODIS (Combined DNA Index System).
There is a better station to analyze that evidence. Try using it somewhere else.
STOP! You don't know if that hair is from a human, dog, or something else entirely! Examining the hair sample under the microscope should be your first step.
If you want to compare this DNA sample to another one, you'll need to drag a second sample to this station too.

STOP! Since the unknown hair doesn't have a follicular tag, we can't extract nuclear DNA. First, use the comparison microscope to find a match between the unknown hair and the suspect's hair; then we'll talk about mtDNA comparison.

Wait! Logically we don't want to waste time and money doing DNA comparisons of the bitemark saliva until we can narrow down the possible biter. Come back to this when you have found a match between the suspect's bite impression and the bitemark photo.
It doesn't make sense to compare these two samples.
Which sample do you want to compare the unknown DNA to?
Which DNA sample do you want to compare the unknown hair to?
Which sample do you want to compare this DNA sample to?
Knox's DNA Sample
Ranger's DNA Sample
Murphy's DNA Sample

Unknown

Hair

Unknown

DNA
The unknown hair doesn't match this suspect. We shouldn't waste time and money running a mtDNA comparison.
You'll need to collect a DNA sample from this suspect before you can do any comparisons.
The bitemark photo doesn't match this suspect's bite impression. There is no need to do a DNA comparison.

The saliva you collected from the bitemark may be our best chance to identify the murderer. You had some experience processing DNA during your Rookie Training, so I'll handle this step. I'll send the DNA profile to the computer when I'm done.
DNA Profiling A DNA profile contains 13 unique genetic markers (known DNA sequences). No two individuals (except identical twins) have the same exact markers.
Drag the DNA from the bitemark saliva to the left field.
Search CODIS to find possible matches.
CODIS The <u>Co</u>mbined <u>D</u>NA <u>I</u>ndex <u>S</u>ystem (CODIS) is a database of over 4 million DNA profiles. The profiles come from convicted felons, evidence, and missing persons.
Drag a possible match to the right field to compare them.
DNA samples are NOT identical.
DNA samples are identical.
Unknown DNA Profile:
Possible Matches:
SEARCH CODIS
CONFIRM MATCH
Interpret Results
Human Blood
No Human Blood
Test Error
Reference Pictures
Processing DNA...
What did you learn from the DNA profile?
That's not what I recall. Try again.
I don't think so. Try again.
Right. What are you going to do next?
Don't give up so easily. There are definitely other things you can do to help the investigation.
I wouldn't suggest it. There is a more realistic solution.
That's a great idea. Good luck with your investigation!
There was a matching DNA profile in CODIS.
There was NOT a matching DNA profile in CODIS.
It is impossible to have a match because there is no DNA in this saliva sample.
There is nothing else I can do.
I'll collect DNA samples from everyone in the dog show to find a match.
I'll start collecting DNA samples from the suspects for comparison.
Thanks, Greg.

The luminol test detected blood in Dr. Knox's office, but is it human blood or animal blood? That's the first question we have to answer. The best test for this is an ABA card. It quickly and easily differentiates between human and animal blood.
First, we need to extract the sample. Click on the swab to start the extraction process.
Now, use the dropper to put the extraction to the sample well labeled with a "S" on the ABA card.

The card contains different ANTIBODIES. Some antibodies bind only to proteins found in human blood. When the proteins are present, a red color is produced in the "T" area. Click on the button to interpret the result.
Compare your card to the reference pictures. What does the result tell you? There is ______ .
No, the red line in the "T" area indicates there are human blood proteins in your sample. Try again.
Try again -- the test seems to be working correctly, otherwise we would not see any red lines in the result.

Correct. Since this is human blood, the next step will be to see if it is our victim's blood. I'll process the unknown blood sample as well as our victim's blood sample and then send the DNA profiles to the computer when I'm done.
Drag the DNA from the unknown blood to the left field.
Drag Marco Williams' DNA to the right field to compare them.
DNA samples are identical.
What did you learn from these DNA profiles?
No -- You found two matching DNA profiles, but Sariel Knox's wasn't one of them. Try again.
How do you figure that? You're jumping to conclusions. Try again.
That's right. How do you know it was Marco Williams' blood?
I don't remember any matches in CODIS. You made this match using another method.
Two similar DNA profiles usually doesn't mean much, unless we are doing a family comparison. Try again.
Well done. Sariel Knox is our number 1 suspect now. It will require more investigation -- I'll talk to Brass about getting a search warrant.
It was Marco Williams' blood in the vet office.
It was Sariel Knox's blood in the vet office.
It was the murderer's blood in the vet office.
I found a match in CODIS.
The DNA profiles were identical.
The DNA profiles were similar.
I'll go to her office again when I'm done in the lab.

Sariel Knox's bite impression matches the bitemark on our victim. Now, it's time to use DNA to confirm if she bit Mr. Williams. I'll process the samples and then send the DNA profiles to the computer when I'm done.
Drag the DNA from the bitemark saliva to the left field.
Drag Sariel Knox's DNA to the right field to compare them.
What did you learn from the DNA test?
No, we've excluded Ms. Ranger as the biter because her bite impression didn't match the bitemark. Try again.
That's not what the results show us. Try again.
Good job! You got an important lead. See if you can find out more.
It confirmed that Deborah Ranger bit Mr. Williams.
It confirmed that Sariel Knox bit Mr. Williams.
It confirmed that Mr. Williams bit himself.
I'll keep investigating.
Mitochondrial DNA, or mtDNA for short, is located in mitochondria within a cell. Do you know where it is?
That's the nucleus, home of the nuclear DNA. A cell has only one nucleus, but hundreds of thousands of mitochondria. Try again.
That's the cytoplasm, all the fluid inside a cell. It contains nutrients, proteins, and other organelles. Try again.
It's the cell membrane. It encloses all the organelles and the cytoplasm as well as covers and protects the cell. Try again.
That's the golgi apparatus. It is responsible for transporting proteins to all the different organelles in the cell. Try again.
That's a vesicle. Vesicles are like small sacs floating in the cytoplasm. They can contain proteins and other molecules. Try again.
That's the endoplasmic reticulum, the location where the proteins are made. Try again.
Good job! A mitochondrion is the site of energy production. Each mitochondrion also contains 2 to 10 copies of mtDNA and each copy forms a complete circle.
Both mtDNA and nuclear DNA form a double helix, but there are differences between them. It's helpful that you know the differences before you start your analysis.
These are some of the differences between mtDNA and nuclear DNA. The most important one is that mtDNA is inherited only from the mother.

During fertilization, only the sperm nucleus is transported into the egg cell, while the father's mitochondria are excluded. The offspring cell contains nuclear DNA from both the father and the mother, but only mtDNA from the mother.
Since mtDNA is inherited only from the mother, a woman's children will all have identical mtDNA. In this (fictional) family tree, Rich, Jen, and Dianne all have the same mtDNA as their mom, Mary.
When each of Mary's children gets married, the grandchildren will inherit the mtDNA from THEIR mother.
For each of the grandchildren, decide which mtDNA he or she will inherit. Use the brush to paint the appropriate color that represents his or her mtDNA.
You assigned a wrong color to some of the grandchildren. Try again.
Well done! Now, lets see how we compare mtDNA.

The human mtDNA can be divided into the coding region and the control region. In forensic lab, we use a portion within the control region to run mtDNA comparison because they are highly variable within the human population.
I have some samples ready for comparison, and I can show you the process when you are ready.

After a sample is processed, we compare it to the "Anderson sequence" -- a STANDARD reference sequence that is used for all mtDNA comparisons. First, the computer compares Sample #1 to the Anderson sequence and marks the differences between the two. Click on "START" to begin the process.
Then, we are going to do the same thing with Sample #2 and Sample #3.
The computer is comparing Sample #2 and Sample #3 to the Anderson sequence and marking the differences.
Here you see the results of the three comparisons. Click the two results that are identical.
Look closer on the two results. They don't look similar. Try again.
Exactly! These two samples have identical differences from the Anderson sequence. From these results, can we conclude that both mtDNA sequences come from the same person?
Now, you can compare the mtDNA from the unknown hair to the Anderson sequence and mark the differences between the two.
You also need to compare Sariel Knox's mtDNA to the Anderson sequence and mark the differences.
Are these two mtDNA samples identical?
Are you sure? The differences seem identical to me. Try again.

So far, all the DNA tests we've done have involved only nuclear DNA. Remember the unknown hair has no follicular tag, so our only option is mitochondrial DNA -- mtDNA -- analysis. Since this will be your first mtDNA analysis, you'll have to complete a short training course before you're ready.
That's not correct. Mitochondrial DNA is NOT as unique as nuclear DNA, so different people can have identical mtDNA. Can you tell me who can have the same mtDNA?
You're right. Different people can have identical mtDNA. Can you tell me who can have the same mtDNA?
Race is not the determining reason if people have the same mtDNA. Try again.
A person's mtDNA does come from one of his/her parents, but not the father. Try again.
You're right. Now you're ready to analyze your evidence. The steps of processing mtDNA are similar to what you learned in Rookie Training, so I'll handle that part and you interpret the results.
I have processed the mtDNA from the unknown hair, and from the cheek cells you got from Sariel Knox's buccal swab.
So, the mtDNA in the unknown hair is a match to Sariel Knox. What does that tell us?
What? You're just leaping to conclusions. Listen to what the evidence is telling you.
No -- remember, mtDNA isn't as conclusive as nuclear DNA evidence. You can't be that specific. Try again.
Correct. Who else would probably have a matching mtDNA besides Sariel Knox?
No -- mtDNA is inherited ONLY from a person's mother. Sariel may be related to several people who don't all have the same mother.
People of the same race don't necessarily have identical mtDNA. We're looking for people who are more closely related.
Exactly -- all of Sariel's maternal siblings will have identical mtDNA. That's why we need other evidence.
Two people who are among the same race group.
Two people who have the same fathers, and different mothers.
Two people who have the same mother.
OK.
Sariel Knox murdered Marco Williams.
This is definitely Sariel Knox's hair.
This is probably Sariel Knox's hair.
Everyone related to Sariel Knox.
Everyone who's the same race as Sariel Knox.
Anyone with the same mother as Sariel Knox.
I'll go analyze more evidence.
Mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA)
Nuclear DNA
mtDNA (1 copy)
Control Region
Coding Region
Click on the control region to continue.
Click on the mitochondrion when you find it.
Click on one of the mitochondria to continue.
NUCLEUS
CYTOPLASM
CELL MEMBRANE
GOLGI APPARATUS
VESICLES
ENDOPLASMIC RETICULUM
MITOCHONDRION
thousands of copies per cell
two copies per cell
circular shape
linear shape
inherited from just the mother
inherited from both parents (one copy from each parent)
Drag the hairs to the viewing areas.
Focus the microscope on the hairs.
I'm not seeing the similarity. The color in Ms. Ranger's hair is so uniform it looks fake. In naturally colored hair, the color is more grainy and less evenly distributed.
Are you sure? They look very similar to me.
Which hair sample do you want to compare the unknown hair to?
Ranger's Hair Sample
Knox's Hair Sample
You'll need to collect a hair sample from this suspect before you can do any comparisons.
When examined microscopically, the three distinct parts of the hair can be seen:
We mainly use characteristics of the cuticle and the medulla to distinguish human hair from other species. Now, use the microscope to answer some basic questions about the unknown hair.
Focus the microscope on the hair.
Look at both light sources
OBLIQUE LIGHTING is lighting from the side, not underneath. This lighting highlights the cuticle (surface) of the hair.
DIRECT LIGHTING is lighting from underneath. It reveals the inner structures of the hair.
Look at the unknown hair to answer a series of questions. Each question narrows the field of possible hair types.
What is the SCALE PATTERN of the cuticle?
I don't think so -- coronal scales are larger, crown-shaped, and stick out more. Try again.
No, spinous scales are smaller, more pointed, and stick out more than these.
Correct. The scales are flattened and lay close to each other.
The CUTICLE, or outside covering, of the hair is formed from many small, overlapping scales. The scales grow in three different patterns. These SCALE PATTERNS are:
CORONAL (Pronounced car-ROW-nul, from the Latin for crown)
Scales look like stacked crowns or paper cups.
Example: bat hair
SPINOUS (Pronounced SPY-nes, shaped like a spine)
The petal-shaped scales protrude from the hair surface.
Example: rabbit hair
IMBRICATE (Pronounced IM-brie-cut, having overlapping edges, as roof tiles)
The flattened scales lie close together and overlap tightly.
Example: human/dog hair
What type of MEDULLA does this hair have?
No -- I don't see much of a medulla at all. Try again.
You're right. I don't see a medulla in this hair either.

The MEDULLA is a collection of cells that make up the center of the hair. Most animals have a medulla that is either continuous or interrupted, while the medulla of human head hairs is mostly fragmented or absent.
Continuous
Example: dog hair
Medulla forms a solid line in the center of the hair.
Interrupted
Example: rabbit hair
Medulla is seen as individual cells. The pattern may be regular or irregular.
Fragmented/Absent
Example: human hair
There is little or no medulla present in the hair.
Is there a FOLLICULAR TAG attached to the hair root?
I don't see any tissue around the root. Try again.
Right, I don't see any either.
The root is the site of all hair growth. A FOLLICULAR TAG surrounds the hair root and supplies it with nutrients for growth. If a hair is pulled out, the follicular tag is sometimes removed with it.
Root with Follicular Tag
Root surrounded by tissue. HAS NUCLEAR DNA
Root without Follicular Tag
Root is club-shaped. NO NUCLEAR DNA
Compare
